messages.author: mikey
messages.content: Although there's lots of hype in GPUlandia in venture, what these guys are doing is a BIG technical achievement that opens the door to ASICs for 1-bit quantization which would not require matrix math... this is deep SOTA work. From their luanch post they rebuild the entire transformer around a 1-bit metaphor. In practice, this paves the way for greater intelligence densitiy by orders of magnitude, this is a foundational technology to enable always-on intelligence.

messages.author: mikey
messages.content: Also before anyone asks -- CPUs are not any more ideal for this thant GPUs are, you would want a whole circuit design based around transistor-parameter mapping = ASICs

messages.author: Sevdat
messages.content:
Good evening,
I had an idea to make a very efficient AI. Its a form of a PNN that uses Predictive coding algorithm using 1 bit LLM Architecture, but requires a non determenistic stochastic hardware to function efficiently.
PNN:
Basically each neuron has a probability of activating or not activating. So in the network we tweak the probability of the neurons.
Predictive Coding:
Basically Layer1 predicting Layer2 which the errors for Layer1 is stored at Layer2.
1 bit LLM:
Each neurons are 1 bit (but activation functions are not).
Non Determenistic Stochastic Hardware:
A circuit where activations have a chance of happening or not depending on a controlled noise.
Biggest bottleneck in this is the activation functions because they need to be calculated. So instead of calculating it computationally we make the neurons the cores of the Hardware that has a probability of triggering depending on the voltage we pass in (Kinda like extropics TSU that uses heat). Since we use Predictive coding we can use multiprocessing between the cores for faster communication between them. Since all activations happen in the cores they are technically 1 bit. We could store the memory in NVME and let the AI pull the neccesary information from it to retrain its weights for that specific question until the answer is satisfied by the user. When the user accepts the answer it saves the answer to the NVME so that it can be reused in the future to retrain its weights when a similar question is asked. This would solve catastrophic forgetting and introduce self development with each interaction. To make the memory access possible we could train the stored memory reference values into the weights so it knows what to access inside the NVME.
I'm not an AI programmer, just a regular programmer. I was wondering if you could create this program and simulate it in the modern hardware and make calculations of how efficient it could get with the correct Hardware.

messages.author: pokisen
messages.content:
8+FP1 8 weights 1 bit 1 + 1/8 = 1.125 , 16+FP2 / 16 weights 2 bits 1 + 2/16 = 1.125 , ....same ratio....., 128+FP16 / 128 weights 16 bits 1 + 16/128 = 1.125 its different weight diversity for still conserving thye 1.125 bit aspect from bonsai . The MSE values reported (e.g., input_layernorm 8+FP1 = 291.0) are measured over the weight tensors themselves not over activations or model outputs
Specifically, for each layer (e.g., model.layers.0.input_layernorm.weight), my script:
Takes the original FP16 weight tensor.
Quantizes it using the given method (e.g., 8+FP1) → gets bits and scales.
Dequantizes it back to FP16 → reconstructed weight tensor.
Computes the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the original and reconstructed weights (element‑wise average of squared differences).
This MSE is then averaged across all layers of the same category (e.g., all 36 input_layernorm layers across the model).
So the MSE tells you how accurately the quantization method preserves the original weight values. A lower MSE means the quantized/dequantized weights are closer to the original FP16 weights, implying less information loss. It does not directly measure model accuracy (perplexity, downstream task performance) BUT maybe all what i am doing is bad and i don't want to waste your time and its nothing like quantisation aware training , honestly i don't know if my script have bugs and i don't know how fp1 or even fp2 could preserve information anyway and i don't have permission to post the script .txt here (edited) Monday, April 6, 2026 at 12:39 AM

messages.content:

messages.author: pokisen
messages.content:
Hello, Well the weight is always 1 bit -scale, +scale
The scale is stored in 1, 2, 4, … bits using uniform quantization of a FP16 floating-point number therefore it is an unsigned integer not a floating-point number.
Upon dequantization this integer is converted back to FP16 by multiplication with a maximum value. what i tried to test is what is the most important between high float accuracy with a high number of scale (so your baseline 128 1bit weight with one shared value fp16 ) VS low 'float' accuracy but with little number of scale, so like instead of 128 weight of 1 bit , like 16 weight of 1 bit for one shared value of "2fp" meaning with the same total bit 1.125 you get more different numbers in it , it's a question of trade off (edited) Monday, April 6, 2026 at 11:51 AM

messages.author: pokisen
messages.content: but like i said maybe what i am doing is totally wrong but i have noway of knowing if it's bullshit without asking you since i don't know the methodology behind bonsai 1bit (i don't know if you have enable file upload but still can't) (edited) Monday, April 6, 2026 at 12:16 PM

messages.author: Sevdat
messages.content:
Yeah kinda like a Markov chain XDD
I don't know. The way I thought is that the current LLM's use a lot of power to run. They also use an insane amount of computation just to get an output. I wanted to reduce both.
Currently a neural network is just a neuron that multiplies its value with its connection weight and passes it to the next neuron. The more neurons there are in a layer the more multiplications and sum need to be made to give it to the next neuron.
What I propose is that instead of calculating it we let probability determine its chance of activating. Instead of computing the probability through math we make the physics of the hardware determine it through controlled noise. For example, if there are W(N) connections then we sum their probabilities. If there are 2 connections, W1 = 0.5 and W2 = -0.2, the activation probability is 0.3 meaning 30% chance of activating. This removes multiplications and if we have a device that uses hardware noise to generate probabilities and we pass in the noise that generates a 30% activation probability then we'd decrease computation and energy efficiency.

messages.author: lolan
messages.content: I don't understand. AFAICT microsoft's BitNet was ternary.

messages.author: lolan
messages.content: 1 bit was more of a marketing term they used (you have 3 states in ternary, but 1 bit can only store 2; you need, on average, log₂(3) ≈ 1.58 bits to store each ternary parameter). IMO, Bonsai 1-bit is also not truly 1-bit, since they use a scaling factor for every 128 parameters. But it's surprising to me that this thing even works. I hope they release more 1-bit models. I am trying to accelerate parts of this on an FPGA, and 1-bit could simplify matrix multiplications a lot (it becomes XNOR gates + popcount).
